data.title: U.S. Futures Edge Up, European Stocks Mixed
data.description:
U.S. futures for the S&P 500 were up 0.3% and futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.2%. Changes in futures do not necessarily predict movements after the opening bell.

In Europe, the Stoxx Europe 600 was flat in morning trading. Norsk Hydro added 6.4% and Orsted climbed 3.8%. On the other hand, Delivery Hero slipped 5.7%, and Dino Polska dropped 3.9%. The FTSE 100 added 0.1%. Other stocks in Europe were mixed as France's CAC 40 was flat and Germany's DAX fell 0.1%.

The Wall Street Journal Dollar Index was flat at 97.03.

In commodities, Brent crude gained 2.8% to $108.22 a barrel, and WTI crude gained 1.8% to $101.39 a barrel. The European benchmark price for natural gas, the Dutch futures contract TTF, was up 2.4% to 55.5 euros a megawatt hour.

The German 10-year Bund yield fell by 1 basis point to 3.095% from 3.103%, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury fell by 4 basis points to 4.401% from 4.438%. Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions.

Stocks in Asia were mixed as Japan's Nikkei 225 index declined 2.8%, and Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell 1%, whereas China's benchmark Shanghai Composite gained 0.2%.

-This article was automatically generated by MarketWatch.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Aave price outlook: X Layer launch boosts OKX DeFi lending
data.description:
Aave has officially gone live on OKX’s X Layer, bringing its decentralized lending protocol directly to the exchange’s users.

This move allows OKX Wallet users to access on-chain lending and borrowing without leaving the familiar exchange environment. https://twitter.com/okx/status/2038422555183214740?s=20

By running Aave v3 on X Layer, users can lend their assets and earn yield or borrow against collateral seamlessly.

The launch also introduces features like auto-compounding interest and permissionless borrowing, making it easier for users to maximize returns while retaining control over their assets.

What the X Layer launch means for Aave

This deployment is more than just a product update.

It positions Aave as a core financial primitive within OKX’s ecosystem, acting as a liquidity anchor for other protocols and applications on the Layer 2 network.

The integration brings Aave’s large liquidity pools closer to millions of exchange users, creating stronger network effects and making X Layer a more attractive environment for developers and new projects.

By bridging centralized exchange users with DeFi lending markets, Aave gains access to a broader audience while OKX strengthens its on-chain ecosystem.

This synergy between Aave and X Layer is likely to accelerate adoption and increase activity in the protocol.

At the same time, Aave continues its multi-chain expansion strategy, securing its role as a leading decentralized lending platform across different networks.

AAVE token price analysis

Despite the strong fundamentals, Aave’s token price has remained relatively range-bound in the short term.

At press time, Aave’s token was trading around $97 to $98, showing minor gains over the past 24 hours but lagging behind its total value locked, which remains substantial.

The token’s price action suggests that AAVE is closely following broader market trends, particularly Bitcoin, which acts as a benchmark for altcoins.

AAVE price forecast

The X Layer launch strengthens Aave’s long-term narrative, but in the short term, AAVE’s price will be shaped by market sentiment, volume, and support-resistance dynamics.

In the near term, Aave is likely to remain within the $93 to $122.81 range unless a strong catalyst shifts market momentum.

A successful move above $100, especially with rising volume, could open the path toward $105 and potentially a break above the upper ceiling at $122.81, offering traders the next target zones.

Conversely, a break below $93 would increase the risk of a deeper correction, with analysts pointing at $89.81, $88 and $76.81 as the next key support levels to watch.

Therefore, traders should carefully monitor how Aave reacts around $93 and $122.81, as well as Bitcoin’s ability to maintain stability, since the altcoin’s near-term movement remains closely tied to the broader crypto market.

data.title: European Indexes Mixed in Early Trade — Market Talk
data.description: European stock indexes struggle for early direction as strong utilities and energy stocks support blue-chip indexes in the face of rising oil prices. The industrials-heavy German DAX edges down 0.2%, with energy-sensitive Rheinmetall falling 1.8%. Banks struggle across the continent as an escalating Middle East war weighs on the growth outlook. Societe Generale is 1% lower in Paris, where the CAC 40 trades flat. Autos also weigh on the index as aluminum prices surge, though luxuries are resilient as EssilorLuxottica climbs 1.15%. However, higher aluminum and oil prices support basic materials and energy companies, helping the U.K.'s FTSE 100 rise 0.1%. Rio Tinto is up 3.5%. Spain's IBEX 35 is 0.2% higher as oil major Repsol climbs 0.8%, while the Italian FTSE MIB slips 0.1%.(josephmichael.stonor@wsj.com)

data.title: Robert Walters CFO David Bower to retire
data.description:
Jonathan Solesbury has been appointed a interim CFO, while Bower will remain available to the business through a period of transition. The company said the search for a permanent CFO will begin "in due course".

Solesbury is a board-level senior finance executive with extensive experience across Europe, the Americas, Asia and Africa, Robert Walters said. After 22 years of senior international roles at SABMiller, he was "instrumental" in the turnaround of C&C Group as its CFO, of Aryzta AG as its interim CFO, and of Greencore as its interim CFO.

The recruiter said Solesbury brings "extensive experience of transformational change programmes" and will work closely with chief executive Toby Fowlston, and the rest of the management team as the company "continues its key initiatives to strengthen the business".

Chair Leslie Van de Walle said: "On behalf of the board, and the group's employees, I would like to thank David for the significant contribution he has made to the business over the past three years and wish him well in his retirement. David has played a key role in both defining and implementing our long-term strategy and laying firm foundations to build on as market conditions recover.

"I am delighted that Jonathan has agreed to join the team as CFO on an interim basis. His strong and international track record of finance transformation, and driving business change to deliver accelerated, sustained and profitable growth, will be invaluable to the company."

At 0830 BST, the shares were down 3.4% at 81.95p.

See latest RNS on Investegate

data.title: Iron ore rangebound as surging fuel costs offset high portside China stocks
data.description:
By Amy Lv and Lewis Jackson

Iron ore futures prices were stuck in a tight range on Monday, as investors assessed the cost impact of elevated energy prices and a pickup in steel demand in top consumer China against high portside stocks.

The most-traded iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE) closed daytime trade up 0.06% at 813 yuan ($117.68) a metric ton.

The benchmark May iron ore (SZZFK6) on the Singapore Exchange was up 0.26% at $106.25 a ton, as of 0706 GMT.

Prices of the key steelmaking ingredient have found some support from rising costs fuelled by surging energy prices stoked by the Middle East conflict.

Moreover, traders and analysts were closely monitoring whether tight diesel supply will impact production in key suppliers such as Australia, although "it does not seem imminent production cuts are likely", analysts at JP Morgan said in a note last week.

Also, expectations of improving steel demand in China incentivised restocking of raw materials, including iron ore.

However, Chinese portside iron ore stocks stayed high despite a moderation, curbing room for price gains.

Ore inventory at 47 major Chinese ports hit a record high at 179 million tons earlier this month before falling to 177 million tons in the week to March 27, data from consultancy Mysteel showed.

Market focus is also on the development surrounding negotiations between China's state iron ore buyer and the world's third-largest supplier BHP , with their protracted dispute on the supply contract aggravating price volatility.

Other steelmaking ingredients were mixed, with coking coal NYMEX:ACT1! down 0.33% and coke (DCJcv1) up 0.2%, respectively.

Steel benchmarks on the Shanghai Futures Exchange gained ground. Rebar added 0.58%, hot-rolled coil rose 0.33%, wire rod (SWRcv1) climbed 0.42% and stainless steel edged up 0.17%.

($1 = 6.9087 Chinese yuan)

data.title: India stocks, rupee, bonds, swaps, call at 1:00 p.m. IST
data.description:

The benchmark BSE Sensex was down 1.5% at 72,472, while the broader NSE Nifty 50 fell 1.4% to 22,492, after oil surged above $116 a barrel and as an expanding Middle East war sapped risk appetite.

RUPEE:

The Indian rupee was off its day's high at 94.63 versus the U.S. dollar, up 0.2% from the previous close, on likely demand from large corporates.

GOVERNMENT BONDS:

The benchmark 10-year bond (IN064835G=CC) was quoted at 96.50 rupees, with the yield up 5 basis points at 6.9898%, as elevated oil prices continue to hurt investor sentiment.

OVERNIGHT INDEX SWAPS:

The one-year overnight index swap rate (INR1YMIBROIS=CC) was up 6 basis points at 6.10%, while the five-year swap rate (INR5YMIBROIS=CC) jumped 9 bps to 6.72%.

CALL MONEY:

India's overnight call money rate (INROND=) was at 7%, and the overnight TREPS rate (INTREPTOT=TCCL) was at 6.6%.

data.title: The man who was once the world's youngest billionaire now says he's solved the stock market. Here's his astonishingly simple portfolio.
data.description:
By Steve Goldstein

John Arnold says he's solved the stock market.

As portfolios go, the one put forward by John Arnold, the billionaire energy trader turned philanthropist, doesn't get simpler.

In a post on the social media service X, Arnold showed that a portfolio equal-weighted to the technology XLK and energy XLE sectors not only has posted double-digit returns in six of the last seven years, but done so when adjusted for risk. A Sharpe ratio measures an investment's return per unit of risk, so any number above 1 is considered good, and he calculates his portfolio had a ratio of 1.16.

Arnold's portfolio notably has outperformed the 60/40 balanced portfolio of stocks and bonds in those years, particularly in years like this one and 2022, when bonds fail to offer a cushion because of the surge in commodity prices. It did underperform the S&P 500 tracker SPY in some years, but unlike the main stock market index, stayed positive each year.

Arnold, who according to Forbes is worth some $2.8 billion, didn't describe why it works. But it makes intuitive sense. There's been rapid progress in new technologies, such as artificial intelligence, that is revolutionizing the world economy. At the same time, as the international order crumbles, there have been two wars, first Russia's attack on Ukraine and now the U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran, that have led to supply shortages of oil and gas, sending prices surging higher.

Louis-Vincent Gave, the founder of the research service Gavekal, has been for some time arguing that a structurally higher inflationary environment argues for a portfolio that is 60% in equities, 20% in precious metals and 20% in energy.

He says events over the last five years have shattered key assumptions: that U.S. Treasury securities can be transformed into energy at a moment's notice, that the U.S. Navy controls the world's sea lanes and that the U.S. is a benevolent hegemon with an embedded interest in maintaining the global trading system.

"The bottom line is that even if the Iran conflict finds a truce in the coming days, assumptions that underpinned policies and portfolios for the past decades are now obsolete," Gave said in a research note last week.

Granted, the simplicity of Arnold's portfolio could be its undoing. If fears that artificial intelligence expenditure prove correct, then the tech side of the portfolio, dominated by companies including Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT) and Broadcom (AVGO), would suffer. Equally, if countries respond to energy-price spikes with windfall profit taxes, then the energy side of the portfolio could stall even with rising prices.

-Steve Goldstein

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Sweden Retail Sales Fall 0.6%
data.description:
STOCKHOLM (dpa-AFX) - Sweden's retail sales decreased for the third straight month in February, figures from Statistics Sweden showed on Monday.

Total retail sales, excluding fuel, dropped a seasonally and working-day adjusted 0.6 percent month-on-month in February, following a 0.1 percent fall in January.

Sales of durables fell 0.9 percent from last month, while those of consumables remained flat. Within durables, information and communication equipment showed 5.3 percent fewer sales, and demand for watches and jewelry declined notably by 7.1 percent.

On a yearly basis, retail sales growth moderated to 2.4 percent in February from 3.9 percent in the previous month.

data.title: Indonesia to loan endangered Komodo dragons to Japan for breeding programme, ministry says
data.description:
Indonesia will loan two Komodo dragons to Japan's Shizuoka prefecture, where it is hoped the endangered reptiles will be able to breed, and will receive some red pandas and giraffes in return, an official said on Monday.

The Forestry Ministry said the animal swaps would increase "contributions from both parties toward wildlife protection and conservation, as well as raising public awareness of biodiversity," adding that the programme was intended to breed the Komodo dragons, which the IUCN Red List classifies as endangered.

Ahmad Munawir, a conservation official at the ministry, told Reuters a male and a female will be sent to a zoo in Shizuoka. In return, the prefecture will send several animals to Indonesia, including red pandas and giraffes, Ahmad said.

The agreement was signed last week, ahead of a visit to Japan by President Prabowo Subianto, who will meet with Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi later this week.

Indonesia is home to over 3,000 Komodo dragons, according to government data. The reptiles are the largest lizards in the world, growing up to around 3 metres (10 feet) long. They have a yellow forked tongue and a venomous bite.

Japan's TV Shizuoka has reported the two dragons would arrive as early as June for the breeding programme. Ahmad said the dragons would be sent after a business-to-business agreement was signed by the zoos in Indonesia and Japan.

data.title: HDFC Bank chairman's sudden exit exposes leadership strains at top Indian lender
data.description:
Key points:
  • Chakraborty resigned as HDFC Bank chairman, citing ethical differences
  • Differences have persisted between Chakraborty and CEO Jagdishan for some time, sources say
  • Slow gains from 2023 merger with HDFC Ltd also pressured top management, sources say
  • HDFC Bank stock has underperformed peers such as ICICI Bank

By Gopika Gopakumar and Ira Dugal

The surprise exit of HDFC Bank's chairman that led to a $16 billion stock rout in India's biggest private lender has put its CEO in the spotlight amid rumbles of acrimony in top management and unease about its underperformance versus peers.

Atanu Chakraborty resigned from the lender, which the market values at $121 billion, this month citing differences over "values and ethics", triggering a stock selloff and a damage control exercise by the bank.

While Chakraborty did not elaborate on the differences he referred to, nine people, including board members and some current and former staff, told Reuters the bank had been struggling with internal rifts in recent years, including between the former chairman and CEO Sashidhar Jagdishan.

The two clashed over the bank's strategy and its human resources policies, said the sources with knowledge of the matter who declined to be named due to the sensitivity of the matter.

The HDFC Bank management and India's banking regulator denied any governance or financial problems at the lender, but its stock fell 12% over three days after Chakraborty's exit. It recovered briefly after the bank said last week it had appointed external law firms to review the claims, and has weakened again.

Investor concerns about HDFC Bank's management come at a particularly inconvenient time for the lender - the Middle East conflict is set to weigh on the Indian economy and dim the outlook for credit growth in the banking sector.

"Investors will eventually look at longer-term performance but if results and stock prices underperform markets, shareholders will ask questions of management performance and seek change of leadership," said corporate governance research and proxy advisory firm InGovern founder Shriram Subramanian.

BOARDROOM FRICTION

Jagdishan, whose term as CEO ends in October 2026 unless extended, took over the helm from HDFC Bank founder and chief executive Aditya Puri in 2020.

Chakraborty joined as chairman in April 2021 and the former top bureaucrat soon began involving himself closely in operational and management matters, an unusual move for a non-executive director in an Indian corporate boardroom, four of the sources said.

He also intervened in human resources policies as a member of the nomination and remuneration committee and, in at least one instance, changed the performance ratings of some senior executives, a prerogative of the CEO, according to another former senior executive.

HDFC Bank did not respond to a request for comment from Jagdishan sent via the bank's official spokesperson.

Chakraborty also opposed a proposal in 2024 to approve equity investment by Japan's Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group in HDFC Bank's consumer finance arm.

While Jagdishan advocated bringing in a foreign lender as a strategic partner, the former chairman opposed the move, arguing against the involvement of a foreign entity in an Indian company and objecting to the lack of a bidding process in a potential investment, the sources said. The plan ultimately collapsed.

Chakraborty, when asked by Reuters whether there were persistent differences with the CEO and if these had been raised at the board level, said: "There is a structure to handling various governance and accountability issues.

"If the issues need a finality in the Board, then they are placed there. It's a well-laid-out process and it evolves along with changing times," he said via a text message, without elaborating or commenting on other questions.

Besides the differences between the two, the relationship of Jagdishan with some other top executives has also been a concern for investors and staff. That concern came to the fore in an analyst call that the bank held after Chakraborty's resignation.

When asked on the call if there was a power struggle at the management level, especially between the CEO and deputy MD Kaizad Barucha or other top executives, Jagdishan played down those concerns.

"Kaizad is a very dear colleague, and I have the highest regard and respect for him," he said on the call, and added that Barucha, who has the responsibility for the bank's entire loan book, "will only get more responsibilities as we move forward".

Barucha did not respond to Reuters request for comment.

MERGER OVERHANG

The board acrimony also adds to concerns internally about limited gains from the lender's $40 billion merger with its largest shareholder in 2023, and about a stock that has underperformed peers over the past five years, said the sources.

HDFC Bank's absorption of housing financier HDFC Ltd in 2023 added assets of 7.23 trillion rupees ($77 billion) but brought in a relatively small deposit base, squeezing margins, hurting returns and dragging on growth.

The bank's lending margin has dipped to 3.35% now from 4.1% before the merger. It also had to slow asset growth to help steady its loan-to-deposit ratio, which rose to around 110% post merger from 86%–87% prior to it.

While the CEO inevitably bears some responsibility when a stock underperforms, the bank's challenges stem from merger-related execution risks, said Gary Tan, a portfolio manager in the emerging markets equity team at Allspring Global Investments, which owns HDFC shares.

Steve Lawrence, CIO of U.S.-based Balfour Capital Group, said the current situation at HDFC Bank was one of "cyclical execution pressure rather than structural leadership failure".

"Markets demand clarity - and when execution visibility declines, leadership perception becomes a factor in valuation compression," he said.

($1 = 94.6275 Indian rupees)

data.title: Upcoming exercise period for the Warrants
data.description:
NOT FOR PUBLICATION, DISTRIBUTION OR RELEASE, IN WHOLE OR IN PART, DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY, IN OR INTO THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA, AUSTRALIA, CANADA, THE HONG KONG SPECIAL ADMINISTRATIVE REGION OF THE PEOPLE'S REPUBLIC OF CHINA OR JAPAN, OR ANY OTHER JURISDICTION IN WHICH THE PUBLICATION, DISTRIBUTION OR RELEASE WOULD BE UNLAWFUL.

Oslo, 27 March 2026: Reference is made to the stock exchange announcement published by Lokotech Group AS (the "Company") on 2 June 2025, regarding, amongst other things, delivery and listing of 54,908,272 warrants (the "Warrants") to the subscribers in the rights issue where the Company raised gross proceeds of approx. NOK 139 million. The Warrants are listed and tradeable on Euronext Growth Oslo under the ticker code "LOKOS".

The exercise period for the Warrants will commence on 1 April 2026 at 09:00 (CEST) and end on 15 April 2026 at 16:30 (CEST). The last day of trading for the Warrants will be 9 April 2026. The Warrants will hence only be tradeable during part of the exercise period. Warrants which are not sold within 16:30 (CEST) on 9 April 2026 or exercised within 16:30 (CEST) on 15 April 2026 will lapse with no compensation to the holders.

Each Warrant gives the holder a right to subscribe for one new share in the Company at an exercise price per share equal to the greater of (a) a 35% discount to the volume-weighted average price (VWAP) of the Company's shares on Euronext Growth Oslo over the last three trading days (i.e., 27 March, 30 March and 31 March 2026) prior to the first date on which the holder can exercise the Warrants (i.e., 1 April 2026); and (b) NOK 0.5130. The exercise price will thus be finally determined, and communicated through a separate stock exchange notice published by the Company, after close of trading on Euronext Growth Oslo on 31 March 2026.

Further information on the exercise procedure for the Warrants will be communicated by the Company through a separate stock exchange notice prior to the start of the exercise period.

Holders of Warrants may either sell the Warrants or use them to subscribe for shares in the Company within the exercise period stated above.

For more information pertaining to the Warrants, please see the Prospectus, which is, subject to applicable local securities laws, available at websites of the Company: www.lokotechgroup.com/prospectus

Pareto Securities AS is acting as manager in connection with the exercise period for the Warrants (the "Manager"). Advokatfirmaet Arntzen AS is acting as legal counsel to the Company.

For more information, please contact:

CEO, Ola Stene-Johansen, email osj@lokotech.no

data.title: Miika Erola’s blog: Predictability as a competitive advantage
data.description:
Enersense International Plc | Press Release | March 30, 2026 at 10:30:00 EEST

At the end of last year, in Marjaniemi Helsinki, we carried out a very familiar job for us in a new way. We erected a telecommunications mast with the 5G radios and antennas already installed before the lift, whereas traditionally the equipment is fitted only afterwards. It may sound like a small change, but this way of working captures our new strategy well: we want to anticipate our customers’ needs.

I have worked in the telecommunications sector for 20 years. Even so, it is still surprisingly common for a single site to involve several different operators, even though the whole could be delivered through one provider. When many parties are involved, the work easily becomes reactive: only what is specifically requested gets done. That drives up costs, makes resource management more difficult and ultimately weakens reliability of delivery.

In critical infrastructure, control of the whole builds trust. When work is planned and predictable, we can focus on quality and safety.

A proactive way of working saves money, resources and emissions

There is no shortage of work in telecommunications networks. Data centres need connections. Operators are investing in fibre construction and renewing ageing mast infrastructure. Fibre networks, fast wireless connections and modern mast infrastructure are also essential for the smooth functioning of businesses and society.

As a partner to operators, we at Enersense also have a responsibility to keep developing the way we work. When we work in a more systematic way, we improve efficiency and help our customers reach their targets, including emissions reductions.

Based on the Marjaniemi mast pilot, we saw that construction was completed faster, unnecessary travel between stages was reduced, and the site placed less strain on the residential area and the surrounding environment. From our side, the pilot required only a more proactive approach with the customer, and even from a single trial we learned how to make the work smoother and higher in quality in the future.

A more systematic way of working improves employee wellbeing

In our Connectivity Business Unit, we have spent the past two years ambitiously developing our field work processes. When the whole chain works in a planned and transparent way, work progresses in a controlled and cost-efficient manner.

One practical change in our operations has been the expansion of digitalisation across our processes. Our new digital field service management model increases transparency throughout the chain. When the status of a construction project is visible to both Enersense employees and the customer, there are fewer surprises. Better predictability reduces urgency and creates a safer everyday working environment. In one service process, we succeeded in improving the speed of work order invoicing by as much as 70 per cent on average.

Systematic planning is not only about efficiency or cost savings. The change also affects the everyday work of our field installers. Our people now have a much clearer picture of what the next day will bring. Deviations are spotted early. Work is no longer about constantly putting out fires, but about continuous improvement. I believe that makes us an even more attractive employer.

By nature, I am systematic and tend to optimise anything that can be approached in a standardised way, so I am genuinely enthusiastic about improving the way we work. The more standardised the process, the more room we have to think differently and develop new solutions for customers, such as the one in Marjaniemi. That is how predictability becomes a competitive advantage for us.

About us

Enersense delivers the essentials of tomorrow’s society. It acts as a lifecycle partner to customers in energy transmission and generation, the industrial energy transition, telecommunications and data centres. The company designs, builds, maintains and modernises critical infrastructure across the Nordics and the Baltics. Around 1,700 Enersense experts work for a more sustainable future, for example, with power lines and telecommunications networks, at substations and power plants. Enersense had a revenue of EUR 307 million in 2025, and it’s listed on Nasdaq Helsinki (ESENSE).www.enersense.com.

Image Attachments

Miika Erola

data.title: HICL Infrastructure increases Thameslink rolling‑stock stake with £52m acquisition
data.description:
HICL said on Monday that the price paid was expected to add at least 1p to net asset value per share on completion, which was expected to take place before the end of June and will be funded by proceeds from recent disposals.

The FTSE 250-listed firm said Cross London Trains owns a fleet of 115 Siemens Class 700 trains operating on the Thameslink route, one of Britain's "most important transport corridors", covering the North-South London commuter axis.

The trains were introduced in 2016 under a 20‑year availability‑based lease backed by the Secretary of State for Transport, after which ownership remains with shareholders. Long‑term maintenance remains the responsibility of Siemens under a direct contract with operator Govia Thameslink Railway.

Edward Hunt, head of Core Infrastructure Funds at investment manager InfraRed, said: "This transaction reflects InfraRed's ability to act nimbly and decisively as opportunities arise organically in the Company's portfolio, a proven source of value for HICL. By increasing the company's ownership and governance influence, we are well positioned to continue generating value from a resilient, high‑performing asset within a critical part of the UK's transport network."

As of 0830 BST, HICL shares were up 0.22% at 118.26p.

Reporting by Iain Gilbert at Sharecast.com

See latest RNS at Investegate

data.title: ZAWYA-PRESSR: Leading restaurant tech company helps restaurants stay ahead with its AI-powered tools
data.description:
Foodics supports restaurants in navigating the evolving F&B landscape with its AI-powered business intelligence tool, allowing restaurateurs to achieve streamlined operations.

AI has been steadily transforming the F&B industry across production, distribution, food safety, supply chain and restaurant operations. Now as more restaurants are increasingly adopting AI-powered systems, these tools are becoming an active element in streamlining day-to-day operations, bringing a transformative shift within the industry. Supporting this shift is Foodics, a leading cloud-based restaurant operations and payment tech company in the MENA region through its AI-powered business intelligence solution, Foodics BI.

Numerous opportunities and challenges have been brought by AI across different sectors, particularly in the F&B and hospitality industry. Today, restaurateurs can leverage AI in various ways whether it’s improving overall operational efficiency, assisting customer-related services in front office, streamlining back-end operations, tackling food waste, forecasting demands and trends, enhancing guest journeys through personalized experiences, optimizing menus, improving marketing efforts, performance tracking and more.

By using AI, restaurant owners can operate their business with a data-driven approach. One of the significant advantages of AI-powered tools tailored for restaurants is its ability to analyze large volumes of real-time data, allowing restaurateurs to make sense of the information and transform it into insights that will help them make the right decisions. In an industry shaped by constantly evolving customer behaviour, rising operational costs, increased demand for efficiency, these tools are becoming more essential for restaurants looking to thrive and stay profitable.

At the forefront of this shift in the industry is Foodics. The Saudi-born restaurant operations and payment tech company is helping restaurateurs stay ahead in the industry by supporting them in navigating this evolving landscape through Foodics BI - their advanced AI-powered business intelligence tool that provides restaurants with real-time, data-driven insights for seamless restaurant operations and smarter decision making.

Over the past year, Foodics BI has been a gamechanger for restaurants in the region, enabling them to harness the power of their own business data to make smarter, faster and more strategic decisions that will drive operational efficiency, enhance guest experiences, increase profitability and unlock new revenue opportunities for growth.

Some of Foodics BI’s key features include revealing in-depth and real-time operational insights including sales performances and customers’ buying behaviour, AI-powered forecasting for smarter inventory planning to reduce food wastage, automated reporting and historical data analysis to identify seasonal changes and trends, all using predictive analytics. Additionally, Foodics BI enables seamless remote management, allowing operators to conveniently access and monitor restaurant data anytime and from any location.

By transforming data into meaningful and actionable insights, Foodics BI is empowering restaurants to operate efficiently, adapt to market changes and customer demands, and scale sustainably in a competitive industry.

Beyond restaurants, AI-powered systems are also transforming the wider F&B industry, such as supporting quality control and safety in food processing facilities by detecting defective and contaminated products, monitoring food production processes and ensuring product consistency, optimizing distribution routes throughout supply chains to reduce food spoilage, identifying functional ingredients, improving shelf-life predictions and more. 

AI-powered tools are undeniably reshaping the food and beverage industry by offering solutions that help businesses streamline their operations efficiently, bring accuracy and innovation. As AI technologies evolve, their integration will continue to redefine and shape the F&B landscape.  

To discover more about Foodics, please visit https://www.foodics.com/.

Send us your press releases to pressrelease.zawya@lseg.com

data.title: European stocks steady ahead of inflation data
data.description:
European shares were little changed on Monday ahead of local inflation data, while investors continued to monitor developments in the Middle East war that has upended global markets.

The pan-European STOXX 600 was flat at 574.98 points as of 0809 GMT. The defense (.SXPARO) sector led losses with a 0.8% drop.

Focus is on Germany's CPI and HICP data due later in the day to gauge the impact of the war on Europe's largest economy.

The Middle East war has driven oil prices sharply higher, fuelling inflation fears and pushing Europe's STOXX 600 toward its steepest monthly fall since March 2020.

The conflict showed no sign of easing, with Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi militia firing missiles at Israel, escalating the conflict and stoking fears of more disruptions to shipping lanes.

Brent Crude surged above $115 per barrel on Monday.

French central bank chief Francois Villeroy de Galhau said the European Central Bank is determined to prevent any energy-driven inflation from broadening out, but it is too early to discuss dates for possible interest rate hikes.

Among individual stocks, INWIT dropped 3.1% after Telecom Italia said it had ended a long-term lease contract with Italy's top mast operator.

UK-listed shares of Rio Tinto firmed nearly 5% after the miner said operations at three of its four Pilbara iron ore port terminals have resumed after Tropical Cyclone Narelle swept through Western Australia's Pilbara region, which helped lift London's FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 0.2% higher.

data.title: South African citrus farmers flag fuel risk ahead of export season
data.description:
South African citrus farmers are reporting isolated cases of diesel shortages as the industry braces for a fuel price increase, potentially impacting the fruit export season which starts in April, a growers' body said on Monday.

South Africa's Department of Mineral and Petroleum Resources has said the country's fuel supplies will remain stable in the near term, despite the war in the Middle East, which has unsettled energy and transport markets and disrupted global shipping.

"While official assurances indicate that national supply remains stable, industry participants have reported limited diesel availability at certain stations, seemingly caused by unusual buying patterns and controlled allocation by industry players," the Citrus Growers' Association of Southern Africa said in a statement.

South Africa, the world's second largest citrus exporter after Spain, exported a record 3.05 million metric tons of citrus in 2025, an increase of 22% over the previous year.

The Middle East is the second biggest export market for South African citrus after Europe, accounting for 19% of total shipments in 2024, according to CGA data.

The growers' association says 95% of citrus exports are moved by road to ports, exposing the industry to fuel supply and price shocks.

"Should controlled selling or limited availability of diesel persist, it could directly affect the functioning of the citrus supply chain," the CGA said.

South Africa's major agricultural lobby groups have proposed measures such as temporary exemption from paying fuel levy for primary producers including farmers, to cushion against the impact of a steep fuel price expected on April 1.

data.title: Govt may need to support airlines as fuel costs rise, stress to worsen: PMEAC member Rakesh Mohan
data.description:
Financial stress in India’s aviation sector is likely to worsen as rising fuel costs and route disruptions linked to the West Asia crisis begin to bite, potentially requiring government intervention to keep airlines operational, Rakesh Mohan, part-time member of the Prime Minister’s Economic Advisory Council (PMEAC), said.

“The impact will play out differently across sectors. Take the airline sector, for instance — it is already under stress. Air India has continued to face losses even after privatisation… and other airlines such as SpiceJet and Akasa are also under financial pressure,” he told Moneycontrol.

The pressure is expected to intensify as aviation turbine fuel (ATF), which accounts for roughly 35-45 percent of an airline’s operating costs, remains volatile, while rerouting of flights due to restricted West Asia airspace is increasing flying time and fuel burn. This is likely to weigh on margins across carriers, including market leader IndiGo.

“With higher fuel prices and disruptions to routes due to the West Asia situation, these pressures will intensify. Even a large player like IndiGo will be significantly affected because of its exposure to fuel costs, and the lengthening of flight routes to Europe,” he added.

Mohan said the stress is already visible and could worsen further if the geopolitical situation persists. “So clearly, the stress in the aviation sector is already being felt and is going to worsen,” Mohan said.

He added that the situation may require policy support to ensure continuity of operations.

“In such a situation, there may well be a need for some degree of hand-holding for sectors like aviation to ensure that operations continue. The exact form of that support will depend on how conditions evolve, but some intervention may be necessary.”

Spillover into inflation and demand

Mohan said the energy shock would transmit into inflation through both direct and indirect channels, while also affecting consumption patterns.

“The way this kind of supply shock feeds into inflation is first through a direct impact – fuel prices go up, gas prices, and other import prices go up due to shipping disruptions and cargo transport cost increases – and then through indirect channels, including the impact on overall inflation expectations,” he said.

Higher spending on essentials such as fuel and cooking energy could compress discretionary demand, particularly in urban areas.

“If a typical household finds that it has to spend, say, 10 percent more on essential items such as transportation and cooking fuel, then it is inevitable that discretionary consumption will come down on other consumption,” he said. “In that sense, there will be an impact on urban consumption, because households will have to reallocate spending towards essentials. But it could dampen overall inflation at the same time.”

He added that higher energy costs would put macroeconomic pressures on the economy.

“As far as India is concerned, the import bill will certainly go up under such circumstances. So yes, there will be a balance of payments impact, and this will also feed into domestic prices.”

Energy shock driving wider impact

Beyond aviation, Mohan said stress in natural gas and LPG supplies could have broader economic implications given their widespread use across sectors.

“One thing does appear to be quite certain — that natural gas and LPG imports are under stress. The situation on that front is clearly becoming tighter, and that is an area of significant concern,” he said.

India imports nearly half of its natural gas requirement, with a significant share coming as liquefied natural gas (LNG) from Qatar, making it vulnerable to disruptions in the region. Any prolonged impact on Qatar’s gas infrastructure could tighten global supply and push up prices further.

“If the impact on Qatar’s gas fields is such that restoration takes several years, then clearly the price of gas will go up, and the availability or quality of supply will be much more constrained, possibly even in the medium term,” Mohan said.

data.title: ZAWYA-NEWS: Sharjah Islamic Bank to raise $705mln via rights issue
data.description:
Staff Writer

Sharjah Islamic Bank PJSC (SIB) plans to raise up to AED 2.59 billion ($705 million) through a rights issue to strengthen its capital base and grow the balance sheet. 

The proposed rights issue will increase SIB’s issued capital from AED 3.23 billion to AED 4.31 billion through the issuance of more than 1 billion new shares.

The new shares will be offered at AED 2.40 per share, comprising a nominal value of AED 1.00 and a share premium of AED 1.40, representing a 37% discount to the bank’s closing share price on the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange on 13 February 2026.

Emirates NBD Capital has been appointed as the lead manager.

The Government of Sharjah holds a significant stake in SIB through Sharjah Asset Management LLC and the Sharjah Social Security Fund.

(Writing by Brinda Darasha; editing by Seban Scaria)

brinda.darasha@lseg.com

data.title: Henry Schein: A Cash-Generating Healthcare Distributor Priced for Mediocrity
data.description:
Resetting the Baseline

Henry Schein is one of the largest global distributors of healthcare products and services, supplying dental, medical, and related specialty products to office-based practitioners around the world. The company has built a broad and diversified distribution network that delivers over 300,000 distinct products to more than one million customers, supported by value-added services and technology solutions that reinforce its position in the supply chain.

The firm's operations extend across dental and medical markets in more than 30 countries, and it holds a leading share of the dental distribution market in core geographies including the United States, Europe, and Australia. Globally, approximately 90% of U.S. dental practices and a significant majority of dental laboratories and international practices are active customers, underscoring the company's entrenched role in the underlying end markets.

In 2024, Henry Schein generated total revenue of roughly $12.7 billion, reflecting long-term growth since going public in the mid-1990s and consistent expansion of its addressable footprint.

In the most recent earnings period, the company's results illustrate both resilience and transition. In the first quarter of 2025, Henry Schein reported approximately $3.2 billion in net sales, with sales growth driven by increases in specialty products and technology offerings even as some dental equipment segments experienced modest year-over-year declines. The first quarter also featured continued share repurchases, with roughly 2.3 million shares acquired for about $161 million, demonstrating active capital allocation alongside operational performance.

By the midpoint of 2025, the firm maintained its full-year earnings guidance, projecting non-GAAP diluted earnings per share in the range of $4.80 to $4.94, with expected total sales growth of 2% to 4% and mid-single-digit Adjusted EBITDA expansion compared with 2024. This guidance reflects a normalization of demand following pandemic-related distortions and ongoing strategic initiatives aimed at operational efficiency.

More recently, the company reported that third-quarter 2025 results exceeded expectations, with sales of approximately $3.34 billion and profit per share coming in above consensus estimates. These results supported an upward revision of the annual adjusted earnings forecast to a range of $4.88 to $4.96 for 2025, highlighting areas of underlying strength in both dental and medical equipment demand.

Alongside these performance developments, governance and ownership dynamics are evolving. Private equity firm KKR built a significant stake in the company and agreed to board representation, collaborating with management on value-creation initiatives aimed at boosting operating income over time. This strategic investment, alongside previous activist engagement, suggests that investor expectations and governance pressures are shaping the company's long-term capital and operational priorities.

Taken together, these developments frame a company that has moved past pandemic-era volatility and is now being evaluated by the market on the basis of normalized demand patterns, incremental strategic moves, and shareholder value initiatives. That context, rather than simple top-line growth alone, must serve as the fundamental baseline for any assessment of valuation and expected long-term returns.

The Economic Engine

The stability case for Henry Schein needs one uncomfortable paragraph, because the longer-term numbers do not support the idea that this is the same margin machine it was a decade ago. In 2015 and 2016, net margin was approximately 4.5%4.9%. In fiscal 2024, net margin was approximately 3.1%. Over that same period, the effective tax rate declined from roughly the high-20% range pre-U.S. tax reform to the mid-20% range in recent years. A lower tax burden should have supported net margin, not compressed it. The fact that reported net margin fell despite that tailwind implies a structural step-down in operating margin.

Operating margin tells the same story. In the mid-2010s, operating margin was closer to the 7% range. In recent years, it has trended closer to the mid-5% range. That is not catastrophic deterioration, but it is meaningful. The business is still profitable, but each dollar of revenue now carries less operating leverage than it once did.

Capital efficiency has also changed. Inventory turnover averaged above 5.0 times annually in earlier years and drifted lower into the mid-4x range during the pandemic and its aftermath before improving back to 5.0x in 2024. That recent improvement is real and important, but it sits inside a longer arc of heavier working-capital intensity than the pre-2019 period. Accounts receivable days were 47.3 at year-end 2024 (45.7 adjusted for cyber disruption timing), reinforcing that the balance sheet remains an active component of the economic model rather than a passive one.

Return on invested capital captures the combined effect. ROIC previously reached into the low-teens range in the late 2010s. In recent periods, it has been closer to the high-single-digit range. That decline does not invalidate the distribution model, but it changes the underwriting frame. Henry Schein today resembles a stable, moderately profitable operator rather than the higher-return distributor it appeared to be a decade ago.

The question, then, is not whether margins will return to prior peaks. It is whether the current margin regime represents a durable floor. Mix provides part of the explanation. The distribution segment, approximately $10.8 billion of 2024 revenue, includes large group purchasing customers and equipment sales that carry structurally different margin characteristics than recurring consumables and corporate-brand categories. Growth in larger accounts can increase scale while reducing margin rate. At the same time, specialty products ($1.4 billion) and technology ($0.6 billion) carry structurally higher gross margins, and their expansion can gradually lift earnings quality even if consolidated revenue growth is modest.

The 2024 cash flow statement adds nuance. Net cash provided by operating activities rose to $848 million from $500 million in 2023, driven in part by working capital normalization following the 2023 cyber incident. That improvement should not be mistaken for structural margin expansion. It reflects cash cycle stabilization layered on top of a lower steady-state operating margin than the company enjoyed a decade ago.

For underwriting, I do not need a return to 2015 economics. I need evidence that margins and capital efficiency have stabilized at current levels. If net margin can hold near ~3% and inventory discipline remains around 5x turns, the free cash flow profile can support disciplined buybacks and modest per-share growth. If margins continue to compress or working capital again absorbs incremental cash, the distribution engine becomes less powerful than the valuation assumes.

Capital allocation: where the cash went, and what it changed

Henry Schein's capital allocation in the last two years reads less like a reinvestment story and more like a balance-sheet and per-share math story. Management has been using cash flow to (1) keep the distribution platform modern and compliant, (2) do selective tuck-ins and buy out minority stakes, and (3) retire stock steadily. That mix matters because the business is not a high-growth compounder; the incremental return for owners comes from operating consistency, working-capital discipline, and shrinking the share count rather than from building new capacity.

The share count trend is the cleanest place to start. In fiscal 2024, diluted weighted-average shares were about 127.8 million versus 131.7 million in 2023 and 137.8 million in 2022. That's a meaningful reduction in a business where revenue growth can be uneven and where EPS is often decided at the margin by cost control and working-capital execution. The cash commitment behind it is real: 2024 financing cash flows show $385 million used for repurchases and retirement of common stock. The program is not cosmetic either. The 10-K notes cumulative repurchases of roughly $5.1 billion (about 95.8 million shares) with $380 million still available as of year-end, and an additional $500 million authorization approved in late January 2025.

The second leg is the balance sheet. At year-end 2024, total debt was $2.536 billion versus $2.351 billion a year earlier, while cash was $122 million versus $171 million. That is not a leverage blow-out, but it is a reminder that Henry Schein is not running a net cash, optionality-rich model. It is a working-capital-intensive distributor with real funding needs, and management uses bank lines and term funding to keep the system liquid. If the investment case depends on a rerating, investors will still want to see debt stay stable (or trend down) through a softer demand environment.

The third leg is where the nuance sits: acquisitions and minority buyouts. The cash flow statement shows $230 million of payments related to equity investments and business acquisitions (net of cash acquired) in 2024, plus $255 million spent acquiring noncontrolling interests in subsidiaries. That combination suggests the company is not trying to buy growth in a headline way, but it is willing to pay to consolidate economics where it already has operating control. Strategically, that tends to be a better form of M&A for a distributor: it simplifies governance, tightens incentives, and captures the full earnings stream from assets already inside the operating system.

The thread tying this together is that Henry Schein's capital allocation is designed to keep per-share outcomes moving even when end-markets are choppy. That is exactly the kind of setup that can produce valuation tension: the market often prices distributors as cyclical operators, but the company's internal playbook is built around steadier levers, inventory turns, cash conversion, and share count.

This chart shows two distinct but related patterns: the evolution of annual operating cash flow in absolute terms (top panel) and the year-over-year quarterly growth rates that sit beneath it (bottom panel).

Starting with the upper panel, operating cash flow fluctuates meaningfully across the period rather than moving in a straight line. There are years where cash generation is closer to the mid-$300 range and others where it rises toward roughly $700. What stands out visually is not smooth compounding, but cyclicality with recovery phases. The most recent bar is the highest in the entire series, materially above the preceding years, indicating that the latest period represents a peak in operating cash flow relative to the prior decade.

The lower panel helps explain how that peak developed. Quarterly year-over-year growth oscillates between negative and positive territory throughout the period. Several clusters of red bars show contraction phases where cash flow declined versus the prior year. These are followed by green bars signaling rebounds. The magnitude of these swings varies, but the final year in the chart shows a particularly strong positive spike in year-over-year growth, clearly larger than most earlier expansions.

Taken together, the two panels describe a business whose cash generation is not linear but episodic. Periods of contraction are real and visible. However, the most recent data point combines both the highest absolute operating cash flow in the series and one of the strongest year-over-year rebounds. From an ownership perspective, the question is not whether volatility exists, but whether the current peak reflects a structural improvement in cash conversion or simply the upper end of another cycle.

Valuation

Valuation is less about narrative and more about present-day economics. In fiscal 2024, Henry Schein generated approximately $848 million of operating cash flow. After capital expenditures of roughly $170 million, free cash flow was approximately $675 million. At a market capitalization closer to $9.09.3 billion based on recent trading levels, that implies a free cash flow yield of roughly 7.27.5%. That yield is the correct starting point, not forward multiples.

Net debt has been around $2.32.5 billion in recent reporting periods, placing leverage at roughly 2.0x EBITDA. The balance sheet is not distressed, but neither is it underlevered. This means equity return must primarily come from internal cash generation rather than financial engineering.

The underwriting question, therefore, is straightforward: what does a 6.57% cash yield compound into if margins remain where they are today?

If revenue grows 35% annually, roughly in line with dental procedure growth and pricing, and net margin holds near ~3%, free cash flow could grow at a similar low-to-mid single-digit rate before the impact of buybacks. Henry Schein has historically retired 12% of shares annually in normal operating conditions. Combining a 6.57% starting yield with 35% organic growth and 12% per-share lift from repurchases produces an expected equity return profile in the 1013% range without any multiple expansion.

That is the base case. It assumes today's margin regime persists. It does not assume recovery to 2015 economics.

CompanyFY Revenue (approx.)Operating MarginNet MarginEV/EBITDAForward P/E
Henry Schein~$13B~56%~3%~14x~1819x
Patterson Companies~$6.5B~45%~23%~1011x~1516x
Owens & Minor~$10B~34%~12%~910x~1415x
Cardinal Health>$200B~23%~12%~1112x~1718x

The comparison shows that Henry Schein trades at a modest premium to lower-margin distributors but not at levels associated with structurally superior businesses. The premium reflects its specialty and technology exposure, higher historical return profile, and stronger balance sheet discipline relative to some peers.

The key issue is whether that premium is deserved.

A decade ago, Henry Schein generated higher operating margins and double-digit ROIC. Today, margins are lower and returns are closer to high single digits. The stock does not trade at a distressed multiple, but neither does it assume a return to former peak economics. It is priced as a stable, mid-quality distributor with incremental mix optionality.

The conclusion, therefore, should not be hedged. This is not a rerating thesis. It is a permanent-owner arithmetic case. At a ~6.57% starting cash yield and a plausible 35% growth trajectory, the stock can produce low-double-digit returns if operating margins stabilize at current levels. If margins compress further toward 2% net, the equity return falls quickly into mid-single digits and buybacks lose their leverage effect.The valuation does not protect against structural margin erosion. It does not need to. It only requires stability.

Ownership

Palestra Capital Management is the largest disclosed holder in this snapshot, owning 2.38 million shares valued at roughly $180 million, representing 6.40% of its portfolio. Notably, the fund reduced its position by 44.98% in the latest reporting period, trimming approximately 1.95 million shares. Even after that reduction, Henry Schein remains a meaningful position in the portfolio, with an average buy price of $70.19 and the position currently modestly above cost.

Southpoint Capital Advisors, by contrast, increased its exposure materially. It now holds 2.50 million shares valued at approximately $189 million, or 4.39% of its portfolio, after adding 700,000 shares (+38.89%). Its average purchase price stands at $72.03. This type of accumulation into the mid-$70 range suggests conviction at current valuation levels rather than deep distress buying.

Lyrical Asset Management, with 1.68 million shares valued at about $127 million (1.76% of portfolio), reduced its position slightly (-4.78%), while Lakewood Capital initiated a new 305,000-share position valued at $23 million. DME Capital Management also established a new position of 469,250 shares valued at $35 million. These new entries are meaningful because they indicate that the stock continues to attract event-driven and value-oriented managers despite mixed recent performance.

Olstein Capital Management reduced its position by 11%, and Kerrisdale Advisors and Steinberg Asset Management held steady, with no material change in reported share count.

The average buy prices across the major holders cluster in a relatively tight band between approximately $69 and $72 per share. That clustering is important. It implies that many of these managers initiated or built positions around similar valuation levels, rather than at extreme cyclical troughs. The current price action remains modestly above most of those average cost bases, suggesting that ownership is not dominated by deeply underwater capital.

Taken together, the shareholder base reflects a mix of trimming by early or larger holders and accumulation by selective, value-oriented managers. It does not show wholesale distribution, nor does it show aggressive conviction buying at scale. Instead, it suggests a stock that is owned by fundamentally oriented funds that view the business as reasonably priced rather than obviously mispriced, which aligns with the valuation picture developed earlier.

Structural risks

The first is operational fragility in a distribution-and-IT workflow that has to run continuously. Henry Schein's October 2023 cyber incident disrupted operations across its North American and European dental and medical distribution businesses, and management quantified a material earnings impact from that event in its reporting and guidance. That is not just a one-off headline risk; it is a reminder that a distributor's moat is partially systems uptime. If ordering, fulfillment, pricing files, or customer connectivity degrade, the business can lose volume quickly to competitors and incur real remediation and legal costs afterward.

Second, the customer base is structurally negotiating up over time. The company sells into a market that increasingly includes large dental support organizations (DSOs), medical group purchasing organizations (GPOs), and integrated delivery systems that centralize procurement and demand price concessions, service guarantees, and data integration. When more of the customer wallet is controlled by consolidated buyers, the distributor's bargaining leverage tends to compress, especially in categories where products are comparable and switching can be executed at the procurement level. This is a durable risk because it is driven by industry structure, not a single quarter's demand swing.

Third, Henry Schein's earnings quality is tied to supplier economics that can change faster than the company's cost base. Like many distributors, profitability depends not only on gross margin on product but also on vendor programs, rebates, and pricing terms. If key suppliers tighten rebate structures, change distribution strategy, or push more direct-to-customer models, the impact shows up as margin pressure that is hard to offset quickly without sacrificing volume. This matters because the company's valuation case is fundamentally a margin-and-cash conversion underwriting exercise; small, persistent changes in supplier economics can alter the long-run return profile.

Fourth, legal and data-privacy exposure is not theoretical in this case. Separate from the operational disruption risk, the company has faced litigation tied to the 2023 data breach, including class-action settlement coverage in public reporting. Even when settlements are financially manageable, these events create recurring costs in security, insurance, compliance, and customer trust, costs that behave like a tax on future margins

Reconciling Stability with Lower Margins

The key analytical tension in Henry Schein is not growth. It is whether today's lower margin profile represents erosion or equilibrium. From 2015 through 2017, operating margins were closer to the 7% range and return on invested capital regularly reached low double digits. In fiscal 2024, operating margin was approximately 5.4% and ROIC was closer to the high-single-digit range. That is a clear structural step down. The tax environment has been more favorable in recent years, yet net margin has declined from the mid-4% range to roughly 3%. The operating engine is demonstrably less profitable than it once was.

But that does not automatically imply ongoing deterioration.

Dental distribution is not structurally insulated from competitive pressure, vendor rebate dynamics, and mix shifts toward large group purchasing organizations. However, the company has maintained positive operating leverage through revenue normalization following the 2023 cyber disruption, improved inventory turns back to approximately 5.0x in 2024, and stabilized receivables days. Those are not the metrics of a business in accelerating decline. They are the metrics of a business operating at a lower but steady level of efficiency.

The underwriting case, therefore, rests on margin stabilization, not margin recovery. If the 56% operating margin range proves durable, the current valuation math works. If operating margin drifts toward 4% and capital intensity rises again, the free cash flow yield compresses and the investment case weakens materially.

The margin trajectory over the past decade reinforces the structural shift discussed above. Earlier in the period, margins frequently reached the upper-single-digit range and occasionally moved above 10% in individual years. That volatility narrowed over time. Beginning around 20192020, margins settled into a lower band, generally fluctuating between roughly 3% and 6%, with episodic dips during disruption periods.

The most recent data point on the chart, approximately 4.22%, sits firmly within that lower range. It is neither a collapse nor a return to prior highs. Instead, it reflects a business that has transitioned from a higher-margin profile earlier in the cycle to a more moderate, but relatively stable, profitability level in recent years.

What stands out is not a continuous downward slide, but a regime shift. Pre-2019 margins were structurally higher and more variable. Post-2020 margins have operated in a tighter, lower corridor. The difference matters for underwriting. If the current 45% operating margin range represents a durable equilibrium, the valuation math based on today's economics is coherent. If margins instead drift below this range on a sustained basis, the free cash flow yield compresses and the stability thesis weakens.

Conclusion

Henry Schein today is not the business it was a decade ago, and the numbers make that clear. Margins have stepped down, returns on capital are lower, and much of the per-share progress in recent years has come from share count reduction supported by a more levered balance sheet rather than a materially stronger operating engine.

That does not invalidate the investment case, but it changes its nature. At the current valuation, the stock does not require a recovery in margins or a reacceleration in organic growth to work. What it does require is that the existing cash flow base proves durable enough to support both ongoing shareholder returns and the current level of leverage without further balance sheet expansion becoming necessary.

The tension sits precisely there. If free cash flow remains in the current range and capital allocation becomes more balanced between buybacks and debt discipline, the equity can still deliver a reasonable return through steady per-share progression. But if operating performance stalls while per-share growth continues to rely on incremental leverage, the limiting factor shifts from demand or margins to balance sheet capacity, and the compounding dynamic begins to weaken.

On balance, this leaves the opportunity more finely calibrated than it first appears. The market is no longer pricing Henry Schein as a high-return compounder, and it does not need to. But neither is it a pure stability story if the path to per-share growth depends on financial rather than operating leverage. At today's price, the case rests on a narrower assumption: that the current cash engine is durable and that future per-share progress is supported by it, not pulled forward by the balance sheet.

data.title: Cravatex Re-Appoints Divakar G. Kamath As CFO
data.description:
Cravatex Ltd :

  • RE-APPOINTS DIVAKAR G. KAMATH AS CFO
